data.title: QUAL: Everything You Need To Know About The Quality Factor ETF
data.description:
This article builds on our ETF educational series by discussing the iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF (QUAL). Factor investing has gained momentum among retail investors in recent years, echoed by the sharp increase in factor-based ETF offerings, making it the perfect time to discuss QUAL ETF.

Although a factor-based vehicle, QUAL has certain detachments from traditional factor-based investing. Therefore, I decided to dive in on key nuances as opposed to merely sticking to the ETF's core attributes.

Without further delay, here's everything you need to know about QUAL ETF.

Basic Methodology

The Fund's Optics

The iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF is a tracker fund, it's not actively-managed. More specifically, it tracks the MSCI USA Sector Neutral Quality Index as an underlying, which is based on a traditional market capitalization-weighted parent index, the MSCI USA Index. Methodologically, the index weighs companies based on three fundamental variables, namely return on equity, low leverage, low earnings volatility. In essence, QUAL ETF provides a gateway to a indexed quantamental approach.

Figure 1 illustrates QUAL's headline metrics. It's shown that the fund maintains an equity beta of 1, which is in-line with the general market portfolio (normally the S&P 500). Furthermore, the ETF has a trailing yield of around 0.91%, which is lightly lower than the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF's yield of ~1.03%. In conjunction, its headline multiples are higher with its price-to-book and price-to-earnings ratios exceeding those of the SPY ETF – link here to SPY ETF's multiples. The factors mentioned within this paragraph and shown by figure 1 aren't static; however, they provide a sense of the difference in composition between the general market and QUAL ETF's quality-based portfolio.

Figure 2 illustrates QUAL ETF's current sectoral exposure. Although its exposure may change in time, I would like to mention three elements, namely 1) the ETF doesn't cap concentration risk echoed by its ~34.61% exposure to Information Technology (essentially on-par with the S&P 500), 2) It would take a substantial shift in the market for its core exposure to drift from its current sectoral weights, and 3) although the sectoral exposure may be similar to the S&P 500's, the stock selection will differ slightly due to stocks having to pass the ROE, earnings volatility, and leverage test before being included in the underlying index.

Security selection is shown in figure 3, where QUAL ETF's top 10 holdings are shared. The quality test is seen in effect where. For instance, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) is the ETF's third largest holding, dissimilar to the S&P 500, which has NVIDIA as its largest holdings at ~7.5%. Another example would be Tesla's exclusion out of QUAL ETF's top 10, contrasting to SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) ~2.30% exposure. As stated before, QUAL ETF's portfolio composition will change as time passes.

In terms of fees, QUAL ETF has an expense ratio of 0.15%, which is a fair bit higher than SPY ETF's expense ratio of 0.095%. Additionally, QUAL ETF has a 30-day average bid-ask spread of 0.01%, illustrating its high liquidity.

Elaborating On Quality Factor Investing & Potential Pitfalls

The core idea behind quality factor investing is quite simple. It's a screening process, whereby investors seek stocks with certain traits (which can be macro, fundamental, style-based, and more). The reason therefore pertains to risk-return utility, whereby it is believed that quality stocks provide more sustainable returns and act as defensive plays during trying economic environments. Moreover, some use quality as a market-riming factor during various stages of the economic cycle.

Side Note: The core idea of factor investing did not originate with the quality factor, here's a link explaining factor investing's history.

Figure 5 shows a long-run performance attribution of World Quality stocks's risk-return, developed by MSCI. Following figure 5, we have figure 6, showing how the quality factor performs relative to the economic cycle.

As previously mentioned, Figure 6 provides an analysis of how the quality factor performs relative to economic cycles. Expanded upon in the next sub-section, I have my doubts about the findings dispensed by iShares in figure 6.

Key Drivers

First of all, although QUAL ETF uses quality screening, it remains invested in the U.S. stock market. Therefore, it is highly unlikely that QUAL ETF would deliver positive returns during a drawdown in the equity market.

For the general equity markets to receive support, investors would need to possess a willingness to invest in risky assets. In other words, the market would need to be in a risk-seeking regime. Such an environment would usually occur whenever investors have confidence in future economic prospects, disposable income is high, money supply is abundant, and the demand for bonds is lower than stocks (in other words, all else being equal, interest rates and the expected return on bonds need to be sufficiently low for the equity market to receive support).

The quality factor component would likely experience momentum from:

  • Investors seek winners-minus-losers. Go back to figure 5 and see how similar the quality and momentum factor have performed. Quality would likely move in lock-step but present lower volatility due to its filtering process.
  • Investors might opt for QUAL ETF when they believe corporate debt servicing becomes difficult, given that QUAL essentially filters for distance-to-default (low-leverage). However, we haven't experienced such an event since the fund's inception in 2013, making the claim difficult to substantiate.

Despite its filtering process and occasional divergence from the S&P 500, QUAL ETF's returns essentially move in lock-step with the S&P 500. There are a few reasons as to why that might occur:

  • QUAL ETF isn't a true factor mimicking portfolio. The ETF doesn't long quality stocks and short low-quality stocks. Therefore, QUAL doesn't truly isolate the quality factor. Instead, it mainly maintains market exposure.
  • As per page S-1 of its summary prospects, the ETF's turnover rate is merely around 21%. Ideally, factor investing requires higher turnover to maintain pure factor exposure.
  • Speaking of pure factor exposure, factor rarely provides pure factor exposure. The variables influencing factors are often correlated. In other words, even if we did isolate the quality factor with a long/short strategy, we'd still see the underlying factor being influenced by variables effecting the general market.

Lastly, I'd like to say that factors are often measured in-sample, providing foresight bias. Moreover, commentary regarding their relationships to the economic cycles are oversimplified – market regimes differ.

Portfolio Suitability

I'm just going to say it as it is: QUAL ETF is basically a SPY ETF equivalent with minor deviations in cyclical behavior. At best, I see QUAL ETF being of value as an SPY ETF proxy, where one could allocate some of your general market portfolio capital to QUAL in the hope of minor diversification benefits. Alternatively, as shown in figure 7, QUAL ETF might act as a minor out-performer in up markets, providing market timers with an opportunity to achieve a few percentage points in edge.

Risk Factors

General Market Risk

The iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF faces the same risks as the general market, namely recession risk, risk aversion from investors, asset re-pricing, liquidity dry-ups, and anomalous factors. To quantify my claim, The ETF's maximum drawdown and Value-at-Risk at the 95% confidence interval are essentially the same, significant devaluations were experienced during COVID-19 calamity and 2022's bear market.

Expectations Mismatch

Certain investors might buy into QUAL ETF's name, thinking it would present pure quality factor returns, without looking at the optics. Unfortunately, history has show that it would lead to expectations mismatch.

Conclusion

The iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF aims to deliver quality factor returns by ranking its holdings according to a criteria driven by return on equity, earnings volatility, and leverage screens. Although it has delivered commendable returns since its inception and currently maintains a low expense ratio of 0.15%, the ETF's performance is mostly liked to the S&P 500. Deviations from the general stock market's performance are minimal, leading me to question the ETF's ability to present pure factor exposure. That being said, it could be argued that QUAL ETF is suitable to portfolios seeking a S&P 500 proxy. Alternatively, the ETF provides a slight edge to market timers during bull or momentum markets.

This article answers three main questions about QUAL

  1. How does QUAL reflect its underlying index?
  2. What can investors expect from QUAL's performance?
  3. What role can QUAL play in a portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Arbe Robotics: OEM Wins Are The Missing Ingredient
data.description:
Arbe Robotics Ltd. (ARBE) continues to be a speculative stock. I reviewed the stock back in July and gave it a buy rating. Since that rating, the stock has been very volatile. The stock fell following my rating and then promptly spiked. The stock was trading at $1.67 a share at the time I reviewed it. It fell to $1.20 within a month and a half in mid-August. It then went on a run to hit $2.70 a share in early October. That is a 125% swing in less than 2 months. It then proceeded to fall from the highs in early October to under $1.30 a share a month later. The stock has started to climb again the last few days based upon news coming out of the White House.

The volatility in the stock comes about due to where it is at in the company lifecycle. As well as the future potential that investors see in the company. The company is operating in a market with a lot of growth potential. It is for the most part a pre-revenue company. The technology is there, but it is in RFQ and testing stages with different companies. If the company is able to win some contracts with OEMs, then things could quickly turn for the company, and revenues will start flowing. This will cause a rerating of the stock and could be a large win for those long on the stock. If the company is not able to win any contracts in the near term, then it will be looking to raise capital to survive.

Due to the nature of the stock, it trades on momentum and news. Most recently, the stock saw a significant spike following an announcement by the White House that it was putting focus on robotics and looking for ways to help the sector grow. These fluctuations are pretty regular with the stock, as you can see by the price chart. This volatility can be great for traders but also has some advantages if you are looking at the stock long term. I have used the drops to enter my position in the stock. So although the stock is down since my buy rating, I am actually up by a decent percentage on my position. I think there will continue to be good buying opportunities on the stock going forward. As I noted, the stock is speculative, but I think there continues to be potential for a strong return on the stock.

Financial Review

I believe in fundamental analysis on stocks. I am more of an investor than a trader in the markets. Arbe is a little tricky with its financials, though, since the value of the company is not so much within the current performance but more so in the future potential. That is not to say one should ignore the financials. They provide insight into whether or not that potential is coming to fruition. The more important item on the financials with a company like Arbe is the cash position and cash burn. I will provide a brief financial recap and then discuss future potential.

The company reported revenue of $0.25 million during Q3; this is up from the prior year, but we are talking about an insignificant number in all reality. It was also a significant miss, which is more of a concern as that means expected revenues are being pushed out. The company operated at a loss of $11.5 million for the quarter. The company reported adjusted EBITDA at a loss of $9.2 million. That gives us an idea on the cash burn the company is experiencing.

The company lowered its guidance for the year from $2-$5 million down to $1-$2 million. While these are small numbers, this is a concern to me. As previously noted, this means that either contracts are being pushed out or cancelled altogether. As a small company looking to start its revenue growth, a cancelled contract or deadlines being pushed out can cause issues with the capital position very quickly. The company maintained the negative EBITDA of $29-$35 million for the year.

The company ended the quarter with $52.6 million in cash. Management also noted that they expect a similar cash burn to occur in 2026. This provides Arbe 18-24 months to get to a revenue point, or they will have to either perform an equity raise.

Automotive Potential

The reason you invest in the Arbe is for the future potential. Autonomous cars are in the early stages. There is a ton of room for growth within the market. The company has also started to see some movement in sectors other than automotive, with industrial and defense applications for their technology.

Arbe has been working with multiple OEMs on integrating their technology. This is the same position the company was in when I reviewed it back in July. This is a long sales cycle, as there is a lot of testing and performance evaluation before a decision will be made for large-scale production. Management noted that they expect a win in the near term with an OEM and hope to land 3 more wins within the next 3 quarters. It would be great to actually hear news of a win instead of potential wins. That being said, the company does seem to be progressing closer and closer to some actual contract wins. Here are some of the headlines the company provided on some of those potential wins.

  • “A major European OEM is expected to announce a strategic program award in the near future, and management believes Arbe's radar chipset is in the lead to be selected as the key enabler of its eyes-off, hands-off automated-driving system for serial production retail vehicles.”

  • “A top Japanese OEM ordered radar kits from Arbe for its Level 4 development activities and approved the continuation and expansion of the project initiated last year, including pre-development activities.”

  • “A premium European OEM is conducting data collection for a Level 3 program using radars based on Arbe's chipset.”

  • “A leader in Artificial Intelligence computing ordered radar development kits for its full-stack autonomous-driving software development.”

  • “Three leading OEMs are in the request for information (RFI) process for eyes-off, hands-off functionality, with selections expected in 2026 and production targeted for 2028-2030.”

These are all great headlines. I also think that if the company announces an award with a major OEM, then the stock could see a quick jump. The not-so-great part about this is these are all just potential also with selection expected in 2026, it means these will be going into 2028 models. That also means revenues will not start until 2027. This pushes the revenue timeline out further. This is one of things that is a bit concerning for me. The company seems to continue to push out timelines on revenue targets. This is partly out of the company’s control, as they are the supplier and are stuck to the schedule of the OEM. The company lowered its revenue guidance for the year. They noted, “the change reflects the timing shift of certain NRE programs.” This theme needs to end, and the company needs to start generating revenues.

Expanding Market

One of the things that has been causing the stock price to spike recently is the news around a push in robotics from the White House. Part of the reason that this spike occurred might be due to the name: Arbe Robotics. Although the company is not actually involved in industrial robots, which was the topic of discussion. This really might have been a matter of having the right name at the right time, or wrong name. I do think there could be potential uses for industrial equipment in the future. I think it's a great market for future pursuit, but, at the moment, the company does not have much to do in this space.

That being said, the company has started to expand into areas other than automotive. The non-automotive revenue mainly runs through Sensrad, a Tier 1 supplier for non-automotive applications. Growth is being seen in the defense sector as well as maritime operations. The company has started to supply radar systems for defense pilot programs and is also in evaluation on other defense projects. Sensrad signed a contract with WATCHIT for boat collision-prevention systems using Arbe’s chipset. This helps show that that technology is legit. There are applications for it outside of the automotive sector. The difference here will be scale, and quantity will not be nearly as high as the automotive sector. Either way, it is a win for the company to have other markets open for its products. The company also said that the majority of revenues in 2026 will come from the nonautomotive sector. This is becoming a growing part of the business that could help offset the slow timelines from the automotive sector.

HiRain?

The other thing that has me concerned about the change in revenue for the year is that the company made no mention of Hirain during the earnings call. The company had noted this as a big win and that revenues would be starting to flow in Q4. The company stated the following during the Q1 conference call:

And we believe that by Q4, we will be able to start ramping up the production in China with HiRain, and this is going to be already revenues for real final production of chips.

Then again, in Q2 the company noted: “HiRain is preparing to ramp up production capacity to deliver tens of thousands of units annually.” Now in Q3 there was not a single mention of HiRain. Not in the earnings presentation, the prepared remarks, or the conference call. This is very concerning to me, as they were the Tier 1 manufacturer in China. They were supposed to start ramping up revenues in Q4 and throughout next year. Now, crickets.

In fact, when asked about revenues next year, management responded, “in ‘26 most or our revenue will come from nonautomotive.” The only mention of anything with China from the company was, “we believe that we will see some revenue from car manufacturers in China.” It went from a big win to a subnote of not knowing when revenue will come, and it will not be a big part of the business next year. This is not a positive sign. Is it going to be a similar process with other OEMs? They are going to get a big win, but then nothing comes of it?

Valuation

The company currently has a market cap of $170.9 million. There is not much in terms of current performance to justify the valuation. If you are comparing it to peers, it's going to be overvalued due to it not generating much revenue or profit at this point. At this point investors are paying for the future potential.

There is not a lot of analyst coverage on the stock. Estimates are for revenue of $8.7 million for 2026. This aligns with management guidance that revenues will not start to ramp up until 2027. Revenue estimates for 2027 are $37.3 million. I think that will just be the beginning if the company can land multiple OEM contracts. That is the risk on the stock valuation. With a couple of contract wins, the company could quickly see its valuation increase. That is the big IF involved with Arbe.

Risk

The biggest risk is that the company is not able to get its product to market. It has a lot of potential awards with OEMs, but as of now it does not have any. The company has cash for another two years at best. The company needs to win some contracts and see revenues materialize.

The other factor here is timing. We have already seen timelines pushed out for the company. If those timelines keep getting extended, the company could still run out of cash.

The last risk is that these awards don’t turn into the revenue drivers that are expected. It appears this is what already happened with HiRain in China. The company touted it as a win and said that it would start to see revenues in Q4. Now the estimates don’t include revenue this year, and the company did not even talk about it during the earnings report. It might not have been as big of a win as investors had hoped.

Conclusion

I still think there is a big market potential for the stock. It is also an early-stage company that carries a lot of risk. I like that the company is finding additional revenue channels outside of automotive as well. The automotive market is still the large potential revenue driver for the company. Management seems confident they will land multiple awards from OEMs in the next three quarters. I think one awarded win can drive the stock price up significantly. It starts to make the technology and potential real.

The value is less than when I previously rated it a buy. I also have some concerns since the last time I looked at the stock, mostly that things have not been materializing as quickly as I had hoped, or not materializing at all (HiRain). That being said, the company has shown that its technology can be used in the market, currently nonautomotive. This makes me feel more comfortable with the technology itself. Management has continued to guide that they expect wins to occur during the next three quarters.

Arbe is a speculative stock with a lot of volatility. If it lands a couple of contracts, it will see its stock price climb. It has a couple of years in cash to make sure it can get to revenue generation. If you are more conservative, then it might be beneficial to wait for the company to actually win some contracts. The price will be higher, but there will be less risk involved. At the current price, I think that the future potential still outweighs the risk. I continue to rate the stock a buy. I will use the price volatility to enter a small speculative position in the stock.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: An Attractive Growth Play In A Defensive Sector
data.description:
Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) sells fresh, natural, and organic food products in the United States. Despite being in the consumer staples sector - a sector that is normally considered defensive during times of high market uncertainty - SFM lost more than 33% of its market value, significantly underperforming both the broader market (SPY) and the consumer staples sector (XLP).

In my view, SFM appears to be an attractive growth play in the current market, based on the strong demand for its products, robust balance sheet and attractive capital allocation strategy. The aim of my article today is to explore the reasons for the sell off, discuss what macro- and microeconomic factors may have contributed to this poor performance, and see whether this underperformance is likely to last or not, in light of the current valuation.

Macro- and microeconomic considerations

Sales and Demand

The macroeconomic landscape in the United States seems to keep worsening, especially from the consumer side. The consumer confidence - which is often used as a leading economic indicator - is close to its lowest level in ten years.

Firms in the consumer staples sector are normally shielded from high fluctuations in the consumer sentiment, as the products they sell are normally necessities. In such times, people tend to cut on discretionary, non-essential, durable goods. In fact, the demand for SFM products even increased.

In the most recent quarter, SFM's sales grew by 13% year over year, or 5.9% on a comparable store sales base, reaching as much as $2.2 billion.

I believe that this demonstrates that the demand remained robust for SFM's products, despite the poor consumer sentiment. The firm's aggressive expansion by opening new stores nationwide also seems to pay off, without putting a negative on the profitability.

As visible on the slide above, it is not only the topline that improved, but EPS also grew by as much as 34%, driven by a strong improvement of the margins.

Looking forward, for the full year, SFM expects a a total sales growth of as much as 14%, while a comparable sales growth of 7%. In Q4, sales growth is likely to slow however, as the growth range is anticipated to be 0 to 2%.

At this point, I also have to highlight some potential risks going forward. In case sentiment remains poor, people may decide to opt for cheaper food alternatives. As SFM is one of the more expensive grocery chains in the U.S., they may lose a part of their customer base to competitors like Walmart (WMT).

Another risk, which may be more crucial for SFM than to its competitors, is the high reliance on fresh produce and natural/organic items. Weather events and climate-related impacts could have a significant impact on availability, quality and cost of the goods, potentially causing supply chain disruptions and margin contractions.

Balance sheet

Over the past year, SFM's balance sheet (see: 10-Q) also materially improved. Its cash and cash equivalent position increased by roughly 20%, just like its total current assets. From a liquidity point of view SFM's business also remains attractive. The firm's current ratio is 1.05, which means that the current assets are enough to cover the current liabilities.

I believe that having sufficient liquidity and less reliance on debt is key in the current market environment. The level of interest rates remains highly uncertain going forward and the decision by the Fed, whether to cut rates or keep them in December will be a key driver for sentiment in the market. As SFM does not have significant amount of debt, the interest rate uncertainty is not likely to effect their financial results directly.

Capital allocation

In SFM's capital allocation both growth and return to shareholders play important roles. Although SFM does not pay quarterly dividends, they do buy back shares. Some investors even prefer share buybacks compared to dividends, due to its treatment from a taxation point of view. Both the expansion and the share buybacks are comfortably covered by the company's operating cash flow.

As going forward operating cashflow is anticipated to remain strong, I believe that both the share buybacks and the expansion could be safely self-financed in the coming quarters.

Valuation

To assess SFM's valuation, let us look at a set of traditional price multiples. The following table summarizes and compares SFM's metrics with those of the consumer staples sector, as well as its own historic averages. If we focus on P/E, P/S and P/CF, we can see that SFM is selling approximately in line with the sector median. It also appears to be close to its historic valuations.

If we narrow down the peer group to firms only in the food retail industry, we can see that SFM is selling at a slight premium.

In my view, this premium is justified, based on the company's fundamentals. SFM is growing at a higher rate than any of these firm's and it also boasts a much better profitability.

Considering the expected double digit growth for the full year, and the continued expansion nationwide, I believe that at the current price levels SFM's stock is attractive.

Conclusions

SFM's lost more than 30% of its market value this year, underperforming both its sector and the S&P 500. At the same time, the company delivered strong financial results, growing both top- and bottomline, despite missing analyst estimates.

The firm also seems to have a sound balance sheet with current assets steadily growing. The current ratio also indicates the liquidity is healthy.

The firm remains committed to growth and share buybacks, both of them covered by the operating cash flow.

SFM also seems to be attractively valued compared to the competitors, considering its rapid growth and attractive profitability.

For this reason, I rate SFM's stock as a buy.

data.title: L.B. Foster Company: Nothing To Keep Me Interested
data.description:
It’s been about 3 ½ months since I announced to a very indifferent world that I was taking a small, speculative position in L.B. Foster (FSTR) in an article with the stupendously original title “Taking a small (speculative) position in L.B. Foster Company.” Since then, the shares have climbed about 13.7% against a gain of about 5.9% for the S&P 500. The company has announced earnings since, so I think it’s time to review this business to see if it makes sense to hold or not.

In case you don’t have your “Almanac of Doyle’s Trades” open to the right page, I’ll remind you that I bought it simply because the company was too cheap to pass up. The market was paying about $.50 for $1 of sales, and the shares were trading at about 7 times earnings. Although earnings was a problematic lens through which to view this company, the point remained: shares were cheap. This, coupled with management’s aggressive buyback activity, presented an opportunity. It’s time to see whether or not the opportunity remains.

I’ll make that determination by looking at the latest financial results and by looking at the valuation. I also want to write about some of the insider buying activity we’ve seen here.

If you’re a regular reader of mine, you know that I’m absolutely obsessed with making your reading experience as pleasurable as possible. One of the ways I do that is by offering you a thesis statement very near the beginning of the article. This is in case you want more “Doyle” than what you’d get from the title and bullet points above, but much less from what you’d be subjected to in a whole article. You’re welcome. There are three things that concern me at this point. First, although the company is profitable, financial performance remains sclerotic. Second, I’m not optimistic about the nature of insider activity last month. Third, the shares are changing hands at a near-decade high on a price-to-sales basis. All of this prompts me to take profits. I think the shares have little upside from current levels, so it’s time to “pull the ripcord,” as the young people say. This ends my thesis statement. If you read on from here, that’s on you. I don’t want to read any moaning about terrible jokes or proper spelling in the comments section below.

What the Company Does

I sometimes forget that people who search for research on a given company may not actually know what that company does. As odd as it seems to me, it’s possible that someone will come to this site and search for specific information about a business they themselves know nothing about. Anyway, in case you don’t want to waste the approximate 3 calories it would take you to scroll to the “Company Profile” section of this page, I’ll give you a synopsis of what L.B. Foster Company does.

L.B. Foster is a “play” on infrastructure and operates in two segments: “Infrastructure Solutions” and “Rail, Technologies and Services.” The former specializes in selling precast concrete forms for a host of applications, including public restrooms, concession stands, and others. The rail business sells the infrastructure needed to operate both freight and passenger rails. This includes all products from railroad ties to rockfall monitoring systems. The rail business represents about 62% of sales, and infrastructure solutions obviously represent about 38% of sales.

I’m of the view that chronic underinvestment in infrastructure will remain a problem globally, which obviously dampens the potential for this business. This is not a “growth” business, and we should invest accordingly.

Financial Snapshot

In my previous missive on this name, I made much of the fact that financial performance has been sclerotic, and that trend has persisted. For instance, revenue for the first nine months of the year is down about 5.7% when compared to the same period last year, and gross profit is down just over 8%. This is troubling because it’s not like 2024 was a banner year for the firm. Revenue in 2024, for instance, was about 14% lower than it was in 2019.

It should be noted that the reason for the drop in net income this year compared to last relates to income tax. Specifically, last year, there was an income tax benefit of $29.1 million, and this year, there was an income tax of $5.6 million for the first nine months of the year. This $34.7 million swing explains the vast majority of the delta between the two years. This year was worse, for certain, with gross profit down about 8.2% compared to the same period last year, but it wasn’t as much of a collapse as might be inferred from a look at net income in isolation.

Also, the share buyback continues apace, and management has spent $13.1 million on buybacks this year, which is about 128% of what they spent on them this time last year. Additionally, buybacks this year are massively higher than at any point since at least 2015.

In sum, I think the financial performance has been soft, obviously, but this didn’t keep me on the sidelines previously. If the valuation remains compelling, I’m very willing to continue to own this, or may even add to my position. That's a big "if."

Valuation

If you read my stuff regularly, you know that I am of the view that there’s a negative relationship between price paid and future returns. In my view, this is as controversial a statement as “water is wet.” It’s for this reason that I want to only ever buy a stock when it’s cheaply priced, and want to eschew it when it becomes richly priced. Please note that valuation trumps excellent or poor financial results, as this case demonstrates. When I wrote about L.B. Foster a few months ago, the shares were really cheap. This is why I bought them. The overall market eschewed these shares at exactly the time insiders (see below), and I were buying. I think there’s a lesson here. I think it behooves investors to buy shares when the crowd eschews them, when people will mock you for buying a company with so much proverbial “hair” on it. Put a bit more simply, buy when optimism about the future is low, and sell when optimism about the future becomes excessive. Put even more simply, “buy low.”

My regulars know that I measure the optimism about a company’s stock in a few ways ranging from the simple to the more complex.

On the simple side, I like to look at ratios of stock price to some measure of economic value like sales, earnings, book, and so on. Ideally, a stock should be trading at a discount to both the overall market and its own history. Since I already pointed out that the earnings are affected by tax benefits, I’m going to look at price to sales, and price to free cash flow. Ideally, I want to see the shares trading at a discount to both the overall market and their own history.

On that basis, we see that L.B. Foster is trading near a decade high on a price-to-sales basis, which I don’t like very much, per the following:

Source: YCharts

Source: YCharts

With Apologies to Orwell

All investors are equal, but some are more equal than others. For instance, some hedge fund managers have access to some of the best institutional research in the world. Some people are simply emotionally disposed toward being superior investors, and some people know more about a given company than any Wall Street analyst ever could, simply because they work at the place. It’s this last group that I want to focus on briefly.

I’ve known since primary school that it’s sometimes a very good idea to copy the work of people who know better than you. That’s why I was initially excited by the fact that insiders bought aggressively in August of last year. Specifically, on August 12 of 2024, L.B. Foster’s Controller, a Director, a SVP, and the CFO purchased about $150,000 of this stock between them.

In my experience, when insiders buy, it means only one thing. The people who knew the business best were willing to put their own capital to work in what they thought was a winning investment. Being on the same side of the table as people who know this business best was very comforting to me.

The problem is that there have been no purchases since then, and last month there were several sales. It’s perhaps noteworthy that insiders sold at or near current market prices.

Management's (Underwhelming) Cheerleading

In the same way that I pay attention to insiders when they buy, I acknowledge that insiders know the business best, and it makes sense to listen to them. In my experience, management are often cheerleaders, but it's wise to listen to their perspectives about the future. It's with that in mind, that I'd like to spend some time writing about management's latest earnings call. The CEO gushed about a 25% uptick in 4th quarter sales. That sounds impressive enough, but I think it's worth analysing that forecast somewhat. Fourth quarter sales in 2024 were $128.1 million. Using the arithmetic not so lovingly beaten into me by the good sisters at Holy Spirit school many decades ago, I know that a 25% uptick on this base brings us to a forecast for Q4 2025 revenue of $160.3 million. This would be an uptick of $32 million over last year. Given that the first nine months of 2025 have seen revenue declines of $23 million from last year, this would be an improvement of $9 million over last year. This would represent a 2% uptick from last year. This is hardly something to get excited about in my view. Finally, I think it's worth noting that the company isn't bedeviled by "adverse weather conditions and unforeseen customer delays."

Conclusion

Ok, let’s sum up. Insiders have not purchased recently, and have started to unload shares. While insiders may sell for a host of idiosyncratic reasons, the fact that two people sold last month suggests reasons that have more to do with valuation than the need to pay a specific bill and the like. Additionally, the shares are trading near a decade high on a price-to-sales basis. Given that valuation was one of the things that attracted me to this stock, that’s troublesome in my view. Finally, the financial performance remains sclerotic. Thus, I see no reason to remain excited, so I’m going to take my winnings and run at this point.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discover - Netflix Merger: A Fair Arb Return If You Are Patient
data.description:
Value Is Realized Over Time

It's been a tough road for Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (WBD) since its spinoff from AT&T (T) in April 2022. Starting from a price of $24.47 at the spinoff, it was pretty much straight down for 4 years, bottoming at $7.52 during the tariff tantrum of April 2025. David Zaslav and Gunnar Wiedenfels, the CEO and CFO coming from Discovery, found a lot of unexpected problems to clean up after the merger. These are detailed in the Q2 2022 earnings call and include not enough content licensing to third parties, limiting HBO Max distribution, too much spending on development of unprofitable projects, and higher than expected corporate costs.

WBD management made quick decisions to turn around the company, and the benefits showed up in EBITDA, which was $4.9 billion in 2022 but has been in the range of $7.5 - $8 billion since 2023. Nevertheless, nothing positive happened for the stock until the summer of 2025, when the company announced it would split into Streaming & Studios and Global Networks businesses.

This strategy soon attracted potential buyers who saw the value of these parts when combined with their existing operations. The strong price movement in the stock since then got it back to where it was at the time of the AT&T spinoff, maxing my Buy call at $8.30 in May 2024 a good one, even if a year early. Paramount Skydance (PSKY) was the first to show interest in September 2025, but Zaslav held out for higher bids, culminating in the recent bidding war between Paramount, Comcast (CMCSA) and Netflix, Inc. (NFLX).

Although Paramount was prepared to offer $30 in cash for the full company, Netflix emerged on top with their 84% cash, 16% stock offer of $27.75 for just Streaming and Studios, leaving the Global Networks spinoff in place. Despite the possible regulatory challenges, the WBD board chose Netflix for the superior streaming platform, commitment to continue operating Warner Bros. with some autonomy, and the good relationship between Zaslav and Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos. The opportunity for WBD shareholders to realize further value from Netflix stock appreciation and either ongoing operations or takeover of the networks business probably also factored into the decision.

Benefits Of The Deal

Netflix is the clear leader in the streaming business by subscriber count and technology with over 300 million subscribers globally and a tech stack geared toward personalizing and delivering content. Warner Bros. with its studio adds significantly to Netflix's production capacity and a 100-year library of intellectual property featuring such franchises as DC Comics, Harry Potter, and Looney Tunes. On the conference call following the merger announcement, Netflix management noted the value in Warner's long experience in project development, which could be applied to extend existing franchises "beyond tentpole movies," creating streaming series like the Penguin from the Batman universe. Netflix believes this will create more opportunities for viewers and talent, preserving the entertainment industry in the U.S.

Warner also brings in its own streaming service HBO Max. While there is plenty of overlap between existing HBO Max and Netflix subscribers, Netflix may continue to develop and grow HBO Max while improving the technology and user analytics. Netflix may also offer an attractive bundle of both services at a lower total price.

Warner Bros. studio will continue to also produce content for third party licensing and theatrical release, which was a lower priority for Netflix prior to the merger. Netflix still believes that in the future, movies will have a shorter exclusive run in theaters before moving to streaming, but they have committed to keep releasing films in theaters.

Most of the synergy discussion on the call was on the revenue side, with the IP and tech cross-pollination I discussed already. However, Netflix also expects cost synergies of $2- $3 billion by the end of the third year after the merger, mainly from redundant SG&A and technology costs.

Deal Specifics

WBD shareholders at the close of the merger will receive $23.25 in cash plus $4.50 in NFLX stock, subject to a collar. If NFLX price is below $97.91, WBD shareholders get 0.046 shares of NFLX, while if it is above $119.67, WBD shareholders will get 0.0376 shares of NFLX.

Additionally, by Q3 of 2026 before the merger closes, WBD shareholders will receive shares of Discovery Global, the networks spinoff. The WBD-Netflix merger is "expected to close in 12-18 months" per the press release. The SEC filing states that the merger agreement has a termination date of March 4, 2027, subject to 2 automatic 3-month extensions if the companies are still pursuing regulatory approvals.

The break fees are considerable. If Netflix fails to get regulatory approval or otherwise does not complete the deal, WBD is entitled to $5.8 billion. If WBD fails to complete the deal (for example, if they decide to go with a different offer) they have to pay Netflix $2.8 billion.

The enterprise value of the deal is $82.7 billion, calculated as follows:

Netflix has secured a financing agreement with a group of banks for up to $59 billion for bridge loans to complete the deal. WBD had net debt of $29.2 billion as of Q3 2025, so about 63% of the debt is remaining with the Discovery Global spinoff. Netflix had net debt of $7.8 billion as of Q3, so by the close of the merger, I expect them to have $68 billion of net debt at the time of the merger, assuming a run rate of $9 billion per year free cash flow, 18 months to merger close, and $3 billion of buybacks over that period. Netflix currently buys back about $6-$8 billion of stock per year. Management stated they would continue buybacks ahead of the merger, but I expect them to dial back to around $3 billion.

Netflix is currently generating about $13 billion per year of EBITDA, and I expect WBD Streaming and Studios to add $4 billion per year based on Q3 2025 results. Netflix currently buys back about $6-$8 billion of stock per year. That gives Netflix a debt/EBITDA leverage of 68/17 or 4.0 post-merger. Netflix management expect the company's debt to remain investment-grade after the merger. They could get back down to a leverage ratio of 2 within 3-4 years of the merger assuming $12 billion FCF and $3 billion of buybacks per year.

WBD Valuation

The valuation of the Streaming and Studios portion is straightforward at $27.75 per share plus or minus about $0.40 for every $10 NFLX stock trades outside the collar range of $97.91-$119.67. However, WBD shareholders also get the Discovery Global spinoff. This business has EBITDA of about $6.8 billion per year based on Q3 2025 results. A comparable EV/EBITDA multiple can be found in the upcoming spinoff of Versant (VSNT) from Comcast (CMCSA). Barron's estimates a fair multiple of 6-7 in a recent article, but that looks high given Comcast's existing EV/EBITDA of around 5. Using the 5 multiple to be conservative, the EV of Discovery Global is therefore $34 billion. If we subtract the portion of WBD's debt remaining with Discovery Global ($29.2 - 10.7 = $18.5 billion), then Discovery Global has an equity value of $34 - 18.5 = $15.5 billion. Based on a WBD share count of $2.5 billion, Discovery Global is worth $6.20 per share.

Added to the $27.75 deal value for Streaming and Studios, that makes the total value of WBD $33.95. Whether that is a good deal or not depends on the time to close. The total return would be 33.1% based on a current stock price of $25.50, and it would also be the annual return in the unlikely event the deal closes in 1 year. If it takes 18.5 months, it would be a 21% annualized return. 2 years to close would produce an annual return of 15.4%, and 2.5 years would give you an annual return of 12.1%. These returns need to be considered in light of the risk of the deal not closing, and of course there is already some political opposition.

Netflix Valuation

Netflix currently has 4.24 billion shares outstanding. With around 100 million shares being issued in the merger, that would take the share count to 4.34 billion. At $100 per share, that is a market cap of $434 billion. Using the post-merger net debt of $68 billion calculated above, Netflix would have an EV of $502 billion. Using the post-merger EBITDA of $17 billion calculated above, Netflix would have an EV/EBITDA multiple of 29.5. This is far above its traditional peers in the Movies and Entertainment industry.

If we widen this out to the more "tech" oriented mega cap stocks in the Communications sector, we see valuations a bit more comparable, such as Alphabet (GOOG, (GOOGL) at 26, Meta (META) at 17, and Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) at 20. Normalized for Growth, Netflix has a PEG ratio in between Google and Meta.

Conclusion

The easy profits in WBD already came in the past 8 months when the stock went from single digits to around $25 as chances of a takeover increased. Going forward, total return is mostly capped by the deal terms, except for slight upside if NFLX trades above the $119.67 top collar price, or if Discovery Global gets a better valuation than my $6.20 per share estimate. This has to be considered against the downside if the deal is blocked by regulators, but I don't see it going all the way back to the pre-merger talk level of $12. Another buyer like Paramount may try again if the Netflix deal is blocked, and Warner still has valuable movie franchises and a solid second tier streaming service in HBO Max.

Nevertheless, the stock could see prices in the teens again if problems with the deal emerge. Annualized arb profits of 21% if completed in 18 months look like a worthwhile reward, and even a 15.4% annual return over 2 years looks attractive. If it takes longer to close, returns are a bit more questionable given the risk. I expect Netflix and WBD management to do everything they can to sell the deal to regulators, so 18 months to 2 years is my most probable range, making WBD a Buy for a fair merger arb profit. (This timing is more conservative than the 12-18 months stated by management.)

Netflix was already a richly valued stock before the merger, although it was somewhat deserved for its streaming dominance and technology. The merger with Warner Bros. gives it an established studio and 100 years' worth of IP to add to its platform. Still, its valuation would remain comparable to mega cap stocks in the Communication sector like Google and Meta when normalized for growth. That makes Netflix a Hold around $100.

data.title: PAVE: Building Gains Into 2026, Why I'm Still Bullish
data.description:
Commodities may be on the verge of a significant breakout. The Invesco DB Commodity Index Tracking Fund (DBC) has cracked 4-year highs as we look to round out 2025. It’s clear that the Fed may allow the U.S. economy to run a bit hot, and that seems to be putting upward pressure on commodities.

There’s also bullish price action in resource-related stocks, namely those related to infrastructure investment. I had a "Buy" rating on the Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF (PAVE) back in July.

Shares are up 7% since then. I expect further upside in the year ahead—a broadening out in the equity rally would be a tailwind. What’s more, global growth, should it continue, is a bullish factor to weigh.

Commodities Lifting, Pointing to Improved Growth Outlooks

According to the issuer, "PAVE seeks to invest in companies that stand to benefit from a potential increase in infrastructure activity in the United States, including those involved in the production of raw materials, heavy equipment, engineering, and construction. The fund invests in growth and value stocks across a diversified range of market caps."

PAVE is a large ETF, with $9.9 billion in assets under management as of Dec. 4, 2025. That’s up from $9.0 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 47 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 0.51%, about 0.7 percentage points below the S&P 500’s.

Share-price momentum is very solid right now, earning PAVE a B+ ETF Grade in that category per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system—it has held firm and high ever since April. In terms of risk, PAVE’s elevated historical realized volatility, concentration, and material significant cyclical exposure can result in wild swings at times (though price action has been steady lately).

There are no major liquidity issues with the fund. Average daily volume is more than 900,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per Global X ETFs.

For an update on the portfolio, the 5-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. Half of the fund is considered mid-cap, while the other half is split between large and small. There’s a slight bent toward growth, though it’s not huge. What’s more, PAVE is a 100% U.S. portfolio, so currency risk is minimal.

On valuation, the ETF now trades at 21.8x earnings, not much different when compared to the summer. With a long-term EPS growth rate near 13%, the resulting PEG ratio is tame at 1.70x.

PAVE: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector breakdown, the allocation is primarily composed of just two U.S. sectors: Industrials and Materials. These are two of the most economically sensitive slices of the market. Thus, much hinges on domestic GDP growth expectations. As it stands, we have seen out-year expansion forecasts tick up lately.

Interestingly, the top holding in terms of size is an Aerospace & Defense name: Howmet Aerospace (HWM). So, there’s sneaky diversification within the Industrials sector. In all, the top 10 positions account for about one-third of the portfolio.

PAVE: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, there isn’t much to write home about. December and January returns have been mixed since 2017, while better performance has been seen, on average, in February.

PAVE: Neutral Calendar Trends Near Term

The Technical Take

With a reasonable valuation and respectable relative performance, PAVE’s technical situation is encouraging. Notice in the chart below that price action has been rather quiet since August. That’s bullish, in my view.

Why? Well, the RSI momentum oscillator has just recently broken out from a downtrend, which may augur for higher prices soon. Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average—it's on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. And with a significant amount of volume by price below today’s level, there should be ample support on pullbacks.

Moreover, we did see a bullish golden cross pattern right around the time of my previous assessment. Going forward, a bullish measured move upside price target to near about $59 is in play, based on the $13 depth of the late-2024 to April plunge and subsequent breakout in Q3 of 2025.

PAVE: RSI Back on the Rise, $59 Target In Play, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on PAVE. The trend is up, while the valuation remains solid with this cyclically oriented U.S. equity ETF.

data.title: Ternium Hit Hard By Trade-Driven Market Issues, But Not Out Of The Game
data.description:
The last year or so has been a rough one for Ternium (TX), as this high-quality Mexican steel producer has been hit hard by trade-related issues in both its home country and Brazil (a major secondary market) on top of a general period of malaise in steel driven by weaker end-user demand.

Ternium shares are actually up since my last article on the company, with the share price up around 16% and a robust dividend boosting the total return to closer to 30%. While the share-price performance hasn’t been as good as what Arcelor Mittal (MT) and Steel Dynamics (STLD) have done, it compares pretty well with other comparables like CSN (SID), Gerdau (GGB), and Nucor (NUE).

I believe 2025 should be the trough for the cycle for Ternium, and a full-year EBITDA margin of 10% isn’t bad for a trough year in this industry, but there are still significant unknowns that will impact 2026 performance, including trade negotiations between the U.S. and Mexico and Brazil and foreign exporters of steel into that country. Assuming that Ternium can settle back into a more normal cyclical pattern, I do think these shares are undervalued below $50, but there's above-average risk here, particularly given that many key drivers are out of management’s control or influence.

Mexico’s Import Sector Is Basically “On Pause”

The large majority of Ternium’s steel sold in Mexico goes toward manufactured exports (cars in particular) and infrastructure (construction and other supporting products), and neither are in great shape this year.

Although overall exports from Mexico to the U.S. are still growing, and growing surprisingly well, auto production has declined almost 1% year-to-date, and other metrics like industrial production have been down virtually the entire year (down about 2% year-to-date through September), and gross fixed investment has fallen for 13 straight quarters, with construction down more than 10% and non-residential construction down more than 16%.

All of that has, in turn, driven a roughly 10% year-to-date decline in apparent steel demand, and there’s not much any steel company can do when demand falls off that much. Third quarter revenue in Mexico fell about 13% yoy and slightly from the prior quarter, driven by a nearly 15% yoy decline in volumes. Careful expense control has allowed the company to post EBITDA growth (from lower bases), but it’s not a healthy situation at present.

The extent to which this improves next year is still up for debate. Ternium management sounds confident that ongoing USMCA negotiations will lead to a positive resolution with respect to tariffs on imports from Mexico, but that very much remains to be seen – companies have not started relocating production out of Mexico to any large degree, and there are still sound economic reasons to source from Mexico, but we’re basically in uncharted territory where this is all concerned.

It doesn’t really make that much difference to Ternium whether the U.S. places tariffs on steel imported from Mexico. While the company has historically exported a small amount of its production to the U.S., it’s not a meaningful part of the business, and Ternium has never looked to change that – Ternium’s focus is on replacing imported steel within Mexico for applications like cars, machinery, and construction, as imported steel still has around 40%-50% share in Mexico (and closer to two-thirds share in the auto sector). Thus, it’s far more important to Ternium that there be positive resolutions with respect to auto and metal goods exported from Mexico into the United States.

A Different Sort Of Trade Issue Is Bedeviling The Brazilian Business

Brazil generates close to a quarter of Ternium’s revenue, and it’s an increasingly significant market for the company now that it has increased its ownership stake in Usiminas. Unfortunately, while Brazil’s economy is actually doing rather well at present, the Brazilian government’s policies on imported steel are depriving its local steelmakers from sharing in the upside.

Geopolitics Step to Center Stage

Despite almost 4% year-to-date growth in domestic auto production and growth in other areas of Brazil’s economy (including infrastructure), hot-rolled steel prices have fallen more than 20% due to a surge of imports. Steel imports are up about 9% year-to-date after much larger increases in prior years, with China leading the way (up 25% year-to-date and up about 361% since 2020), and local producers simply cannot compete on price with these imports.

Thus far Brazil’s government has done little. While there's a Tariff Rate Quota system in place, it faces severe challenges, as it exempts steel from free-trade zones and countries that have bilateral trade agreements with Brazil.

More pragmatically, the Brazilian government is loathe to antagonize China, and that’s understandable given that almost 30% of Brazil’s exports go to China now, and Brazil has been more than happy to step up with exports of soybeans, animal proteins, and other agricultural products in response to trade frictions between the U.S. and China.

Even though there are investigations underway on alleged anti-dumping cases that would pertain to about 75% of Usiminas' portfolio, I’m not getting my hopes up at this point. The Brazilian government already kicked the can down the road on a decision on cold-rolled and pre-painted imports from China that was supposed to be made in mid-November, and the ministry could wait as long as early 2027 before issuing rulings.

Maybe the Brazilian government is just hoping the problem goes away, but I’m not optimistic. China hasn’t been adhering to WTO rules for quite some time now, and in some respects they need to dump their steel on the global market – they produce more than half of the world’s steel (and it employs around 3.4M people), but they consume about 48% of global steel and that number is falling due to weakness in the property sector and companies shifting machinery production out of China.

Ternium Deepens Its Commitment To Brazil

Nevertheless, Ternium remains committed to this market. Early in November the company acquired the Usiminas stakes held by Nippon Steel and Mitsubishi for about $315M (paying more than double the market price because of pre-existing purchase agreements), taking their ownership from 51.5% to 83.1%, with the broader T/T Group now owning almost 93% of the company.

It’s unclear if Ternium triggered a buy clause or Nippon Steel triggered its put option, but judging by the commentary of Nippon Steel (saying they don’t expect a meaningful recovery in Brazil), I think they triggered the deal.

While this move will simplify management at Usiminas and accelerate decision making (there won’t be protracted discussions around strategy, capex, et al.), it does consume about half of Ternium’s net cash on hand, and Usiminas is going to need some capex reinvestment down the road (especially for its coke batteries and mining assets).

The Outlook

While cautious on the next 12 months, I do think it is more likely than not that Ternium is past the worst of the cycle. Still, a lot of the company’s rebound is tied to geopolitics at this point; I still believe in the long-term potential of Mexico as a key manufacturing and export hub, and I still believe in Ternium’s prospects for replacing imported steel within Mexico’s manufacturing and infrastructure sectors, but a lot rides on those trade negotiations.

Likewise with Brazil. I don’t think that the Brazilian government is willing to gut its domestic steel industry to appease China, but I do have real concerns that the government will do less (and perhaps far less) than it could in order to preserve its overall trade relationships with China.

With that, I’m not looking for a big rebound. This year is likely to end with Ternium’s revenue falling around 12% yoy, and I expect a modest mid-single-digit recovery in 2026 and 2027 and long-term growth in the low single-digits.

Ternium is a very efficient producer when volumes are healthy, and I believe management will meet, and likely exceed, its $150/ton target for steel EBITDA when volumes recover (the most recent quarter saw steel EBITDA of $104/ton; still a good number relative to many steel companies).

With that, I still expect long-term FCF margins to average out in the high single digits, supporting strong growth off of a low base. That said, FCF will likely be negative next year as well as the company wraps up a capex expansion program, and it will likely cap dividend growth in the near term.

Ternium looks meaningfully undervalued on discounted cash flow (with a fair value in the high-$50’s) provided the next ten years look at least something like the company’s long-term performance. While discounted cash flow is a tricky way to value cyclical companies like Ternium (where visibility beyond a year or two is minimal), I have noticed that buying in at a steep discount to assumptions predicated on returns to long-term averages works more often than not.

Turning to a multiples-based approach, a 4x multiple on blended year-ahead and full-cycle EBITDA gets me to a fair value of about $50.

The Bottom Line

Generally speaking it works out well if you buy well-run cyclical commodity companies in the throes of their downturns, and that would seem to be where Ternium is at now. Of course there are no guarantees that the future will resemble the past, but the need for steel isn’t going away, Ternium remains a highly efficient operator with attractive market/share growth potential, and the arguments for free global trade are still sound. It's likely going to take a while longer for Ternium’s numbers to recover, but I do think today’s price is attractive.

data.title: Praxis Precision Medicines: Latest Study Wins Sets Up 2 CNS Drug Approvals In 2026
data.description:
Investment Overview - Praxis Caps Off Incredible Year With Another Study Success

Praxis Precision Medicines, Inc. (PRAX) stock is soaring in trading today, up 35% at time of writing, with shares reaching a value of $260, their highest value since the beginning of 2022. Current market cap is ~$6.5bn.

The catalyst for gains appears to be news shared yesterday from Praxis' EMBOLD study, evaluating its candidate relutrigine for the treatment of patients with SCN2A and SCN8A developmental and epileptic encephalopathies ("DEEs") - according to a press release:

SCN2A and SCN8A DEEs are devastating conditions with extremely high mortality due to the debilitating seizure burden they impose on patients, and there are currently no approved treatment options.

Praxis appears not have shared any specific data, rather news that successful interim analysis of the study data has triggered "an early stop for efficacy." In other words, study investigators have declared it a success.

There was further good news to share in that Praxis confirmed that, following a meeting with the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA"), to discuss its path to a New Drug Application ("NDA") submission for another lead asset, ulixacaltamide, indicated for essential tremor, it had:

gained alignment from the agency on the content of the NDA and expects to complete its NDA submission in early 2026.

Praxis completed a $190m IPO at a price of ~$17 in October 2020, and was initially focused on candidate PRAX-114, indicated for major depressive disorder ("MDD"). The drug did not live up to expectations, however, and was dropped following a Phase 2/3 study fail.

Relutrigine is only one of three late stage assets in Praxis' "Cerebrum" small molecule platform pipeline (its second platform, "Solidus," develops antisense, oligonucleotide drug candidates), the others being vormatigine, and ulixacaltamide, as shown in the pipeline overview below (taken from an investor presentation).

In October, Praxis shared positive topline data from two Phase 3 studies of ulixacaltamide in essential tremor ("ET"), in which, according to a press release:

  • Patients treated with ulixacaltamide in the parallel-group study (Study 1) showed a mean improvement from baseline in the Modified Activities of Daily Living 11 at Week 8, the pre-specified primary endpoint, of 4.3 points (p<0.0001).
  • All key secondary endpoints in Study 1 - rate of disease improvement over 12 weeks, PGI-C and CGI-S - were also statistically significant (p<0.001).
  • Study 2 met its pre-specified primary endpoint, with patients showing superior maintenance of effect while on ulixacaltamide versus placebo during the randomized-withdrawal phase (p=0.0369).
  • The first key secondary endpoint in Study 2 - rate of disease improvement during the randomized-withdrawal phase - also demonstrated superior effect in ulixacaltamide treated patients versus placebo (p=0.0042).

On that news, Praxis stock jumped in value from ~$55 per share, to $190 per share, a gain of ~245%. All in all, then, it has been an excellent year for Praxis, with shares up 229% year-to-date, and up ~500% in a six months basis.

Can Praxis Capitalize On Study Wins & Bring Drugs To Market?

Praxis believes that relutrigine - a sodium channel modulator - addresses a market of ~200k patients, worth ~$3bn.

I have not yet discussed vormatrigine, also a sodium channel modulator, in four late stage studies, one of which - a Phase 2/3 - will read out data in focal onset seizures and generalized epilepsy, a market of 3.5m patients, worth >$2.5bn in value, in 1H 2026, management says.

Regarding relutrigine, Praxis says that its drug has "potential for class leading efficacy and tolerability." It is administered orally or via gastrojejunostomy ("G/J") tube, and it's mechanism of action ("MoA") is discussed below:

Data from Cohort 1 of the EMBOLD study demonstrated "sustained seizure reduction with continued exposure on top of standard-of-care" ("SOC"), with no drug related serious adverse treatment events, and improvements noted both in the clinical and daily life settings.

The EMERALD study of relutrigine is ongoing, with data due next year potentially opening up a larger patient population, but it seems Praxis will submit its NDA ahead of those results. Analysts have "conservatively" forecast peak revenues of $650m by 2037.

Meanwhile, management estimates ~7m people suffer from ET, which carries a "high psychosocial burden," and is "inadequately managed and undertreated." Apparently, ulixacaltamide works through "precision modulation of tremor circuits through T-type calcium channel modulation," and is the first drug to record a positive result in a Phase 3 study.

As we can see above, Praxis believes all of its hypotheses around the safety and efficacy of ulixacaltamide support approval - from a safety perspective, the company says that the most common (≥10%) adverse events in patients were constipation, dizziness, euphoric mood, brain fog, headache, paraesthesia and insomnia.

In fairness to Praxis, you cannot ask for much more than meeting all endpoints in two separate pivotal studies, plus positive feedback from the FDA, therefore, we may speculate the market is right to be sending Praxis stock skywards.

The company could submit two NDA's next year, with compelling study evidence (we don't know what the latest relutrigine data were, but we know they were good enough to halt the study early), one for a drug that may earn ~$650m in peak revenues and the second for a drug analysts have suggested could earn as much as ~$3bn per annum.

For good measure, there will be pivotal study results for vormatrigine, also, another drug that may have "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) sales potential. If approved, all three drugs would be awarded many years of patent protection, meaning premium price points can be applied. And, a final plus, data from its ASO platform, in the form of elsunersen in SCN2A GoF (Gain-of-Function) indications.

Analysis - Why I'd Expect More Price Volatility In 2026

Clearly, the optimal time to buy Praxis stock would have been six months ago, but do shares still present good value today?

I'm somewhat conflicted - central nervous system diseases are highly complex and patients perhaps react differently and subjectively to different drugs, making it hard to understand which drug candidates may succeed, and which won't.

I have used Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Cobenfy a few time in notes this year to illustrate my point. The combination therapy was guided through clinical studies by Karuna Therapeutics, who achieved a resounding success in schizophrenia, prompting BMY to acquire Karuna for ~$14bn.

Analysts initially felt Cobenfy would command nearly $6bn per annum in peak revenues, but then, under BMY's stewardship, the drug failed to meet endpoints in a study as an adjunctive therapy in patients with inadequately controlled schizophrenia, and those peak revenue expectations were slashed to ~$2.6bn. The drug earned $10m of revenues in its first full quarter on the market, in Q3.

For me, Praxis must surely have placed itself in the M&A "shop window" in 2025 with a series of positive data readouts, and if BMY was prepared to pay $14bn to gain access to a single drug, what might a Big Pharma pay for three, plus an ASO platform? More than Praxis' current valuation of $6.2bn, seems likely.

Then again, does the subsequent failure of Cobenfy create doubts around biotechs touting breakthrough study wins in CNS indications? Investors are often searching for signs that a drug will secure approval, and be a commercial success, but indications that might be the case - i.e., Praxis strong study results - don't always play out that way in reality. For more information on the ulixacaltamide studies, this recent discussion transcript provides some intriguing insights.

My take, based on the fact there are still uncertainties in play, is that Praxis as an acquisition target is either worth substantially more than $6.2bn, or substantially less.

In 2025, to the end of Q3, the company reported a net loss of $214m, or $(9.97) per share, and a cash plus marketable securities position of ~$390m, so my guess would be that the company may try to raise funds imminently, to make sure it can fund all of its studies, plus two regulatory submissions, plus, ten months later, potentially, two commercial launches.

Would it be a struggle for Praxis, with <150 staff, to successfully secure approval for and launch two drugs into competitive markets, albeit markets where unmet need remains high? In my opinion, it would be, so can the Boston biotech attract a commercial partner, or secure an exit to a Big Pharma?

Reviewing the data, the alignment with the FDA, the MoAs, which are recognized and used in other commercial drugs - I believe Vertex' (VRTX) pain therapeutic Jounavx is also a sodium channel blocker - I see plenty of evidence to support a bull case. However, because it may take many years, and hundreds of millions of dollars spent on SG&A, to extract the full benefit from these drugs commercially, I am remaining on the sidelines, although I intend to monitor for dips in the share price, and provided they don't undermine my bull case, considering buying Praxis stock at that stage.

data.title: Veeco Instruments: Is It Worth Hanging Around, Or Is It Time To Go?
data.description:
The wafer fabrication equipment business is a brutal one. Not only do companies have to deal with stiff competition from entrenched majors like Applied Materials (AMAT), ASML (ASML), Lam Research (LRCX), and Tokyo Electron (OTCPK:TOELY), the sharp cyclicality of the industry and the need to constantly reinvest in R&D only adds to the pressure.

With that, those companies good enough to make it through a cycle or two often find they need to either pursue M&A to broaden their portfolio or accept an acquisition offer and become a part of somebody else’s growth story. Such is the case today with Veeco Instruments (VECO), which itself got to this point in its story in part through the successful acquisition of Ultratech back in 2017.

Up almost 30% since my last update, Veeco investors have to decide whether to hold on and accept shares in Axcelis Technologies (ACLS) in exchange for their Veeco shares, or sell ahead of the deal close and move to other ideas. Given the relative valuation and how I see the deal shaping up over time, as a Veeco shareholder I’m leaning toward making my exit.

Veeco, Like Many, Is Just Muddling Through Right Now

Like many other players in the WFE space, Veeco is muddling through a trough in orders that has been exacerbated by geopolitical squabbling over tariffs and technology transfer. Third quarter results reflected those challenges, with revenue down 10% year over year and flat sequentially, with both the Semiconductor and Compound Semiconductor businesses down both annually (down 5% and down 31%, respectively) and sequentially (down 5% and down 21%, respectively).

While Veeco has built a strong niche for itself in laser annealing equipment (a front-end process in semiconductor manufacturing that lowers silicon resistance and activates dopants to provide a sound foundation for the chip to be built), as well as ion beam etch/deposition, wet processing, and metal organic chemical vapor deposition (or MOCVD), the reality is that fabs simply aren’t ordering now, and the one area that’s comparatively healthier today (high-bandwidth memory) is one where the company is not yet as well-established.

Weaker revenue has had a negative impact on Veeco’s margins, but I do think it’s worth noting that this trough has been gentler than in the past – the company has remained profitable with a trough adjusted operating margin of 13.9% versus losses in past downcycles (like 2019) and free cash flow positive.

I believe this is a byproduct of a less severe cycle, but also management’s efforts to diversify the business and focus more on “mission critical” offerings for leading-edge processes that are somewhat less vulnerable to cyclical swings, including the company’s laser annealing for leading-edge nodes and repositioning the ion beam etch and MOCVD businesses for EUV litho mask blanks and GaN chip fabrication, respectively.

Gross margin declined about two points year over year and 70bp quarter over quarter in the third quarter (to 41.9%), while operating income fell 25% yoy with margin down about three points to 13.9% (sequentially the business was flat, with flat margins).

Veeco is likely looking at a couple more quarters of similar performance ahead of improving results later in 2026 as more evaluation programs convert to orders in annealing, overall order activity improves, and the hard drive business sees some recovery.

Axcelis Steps In Near The End Of The Trough

The “just wait for the recovery” thesis for Veeco got interrupted at the start of October when Axcelis and Veeco announced a friendly merger that will see Axcelis acquire Veeco for 0.3575 shares. Axcelis shareholders will own 58% of the combined company, and the Axcelis CEO will run the business after the deal. At the time of the deal, the offer represented a roughly 15% premium for Veeco shareholders, though ACLS shares have been weak since the announcement, falling about 15% while many large WFE companies have seen their shares rise by high-teens percentages or more.

To be blunt, I don’t really like this deal all that much.

Axcelis is a specialist in ion implantation, and while it has leverage to growth opportunities in silicon carbide (or SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) chips, the reality is that the bulk of the company’s business today is in older legacy nodes (management’s own quarterly presentations highlight this, with only 5% of shipments in Q3’25 classified as “advanced” logic or memory) and it doesn’t offer real exposure to leading-edge logic or HBM, as the company just doesn’t have much traction here.

While it is true that SiC (and GaN) are long-term growth opportunities, the recent downturn (particularly relative to the strength in leading-edge logic) highlights the risks of not having leading-edge exposure. Moreover, the company has been losing share to Applied Materials in ion implant and it’s unclear to me that Axcelis is really ideally-placed to take advantage of the SiC/GaN opportunities even when they do accelerate.

While management has talked about cross-selling (Taiwan Semi (TSM) is a long-time Veeco customer but doesn’t order from Axcelis) and offering integrated solutions combining front-end ion implant from ACLS and annealing from Veeco, I think there’s a real risk that customers simply won’t see the value in the ACLS offerings (though I don’t completely dismiss the idea that Veeco could at least get them through the door and get a sincere look at their tech).

I likewise have my doubts about the real synergies in the wide bandgap materials businesses (equipment for SiC, GaN, and GaN-on-SiC) and between ion implant and ion beam equipment. While dealing with a smaller number of suppliers can be beneficial, the reality is that leading-edge fabs are not going to be willing to sacrifice chip yields just for some incremental convenience.

I also feel like the strategies and philosophies at the two companies are different. Veeco has been actively repositioning itself toward a greater focus on equipment for leading-edge processes, including its nanosecond annealing for logic, ion beam deposition for HBM, wet processing for advanced packaging, and MOCVD for GaN-based power semiconductors.

Axcelis has much less exposure to leading-edge logic and memory and is stronger in legacy nodes. Moreover, while the company should be well-placed to leverage growth in SiC and GaN, this business has been slow to develop for Axcelis and there is low visibility across the SiC space now (I believe it’s a “when, not if” proposition, but that “when” could be longer than expected).
