While I do think there will be some operating synergies with the two companies, it’ll take a couple of years for those synergies to emerge and I’m worried about the challenges involved in managing this expanded enterprise – Axcellis management has been focused on ion implantation for its history and will have to manage a much broader array of businesses and customers while also maintaining hard-won performance leadership in areas like annealing.

The Value

I realize this article comes off as quite negative on Axcelis, but that’s not really my intention or position on the company. Rather, I just think Axcelis isn’t particularly well-placed for this phase of the cycle and I think there is above-average integration and execution risk with this deal.

Looking at the growth I expect for both companies over the next couple of years, and adding in a couple of percentage points of sales synergy by 2027 and around 4% opex synergies, and then using my discounted cash flow and margin-driven EV/revenue and P/E approaches (including a 2.05x forward revenue multiple based on 18%+ adjusted EBITDA margins and a 17.5x multiple on earnings of $5.56), I get a value range of $77 to $97 for Axcelis and a corresponding $27.50 to $34.68 range for Veeco shares. With Veeco already in the middle of that range, even a relatively bullish outcome for the combined companies would only offer around 10% of upside while integration issues or weaker synergies would create more downside risk.

As far as upside risks to the thesis, basically consider the opposite to my major points. Perhaps Veeco will open doors to Axcelis and get customers to take a closer look at their offerings. Likewise, perhaps there will be more value and interest in integrated solutions combining ion implant and annealing. It’s also possible that the combination of the two companies’ respective wide bandgap capabilities (in implant and MOCVD) will lead to a more integrated solutions and/or sales synergies between existing customers, particularly given Axcelis’s existing presence in the SiC space, and Veeco’s early-stage efforts in GaN-on-SiC

The biggest driver in an upside scenario would be revenue growth, particularly if TSMC would start buying systems from Axcelis and if the combination accelerates interest Veeco’s MOCVD offerings for GaN. Strong incremental revenue growth would drive stronger margins and cash flows, perhaps supporting a fair value comfortably above $100.

The Bottom Line

The good and bad of modeling and valuation is that you can make the numbers tell you whatever you want if you torture them long enough. In other words, if you think that Axcelis should actually be worth more on the order of $95 to $110, more power to you, but I think there are still real questions about the strategic logic of this deal and the likelihood that the hoped-for benefits will materialize. In other words, Axcelis could be worth those higher numbers as a standalone but still lose value if the deal doesn’t work out.

I actually hope I’m wrong; as I said, I like Axcelis on its own merits and I think it is plausible that recoveries in SiC and growth in GaN will do meaningful good for the company’s long-term prospects. I’ve seen what happens when deals don’t produce the hoped-for benefits, though, and I see an elevated risk of that happening here. Shareholders don’t have to decide right away, as the deal isn’t expected to close until the second half of 2026, and that provides a little more time to assess Axcelis’s positioning in what should be a recovering market. That said, I’m still leaning heavily toward selling my Veeco shares and moving on to other ideas.

data.title: Roche May Be Riding A Bit Too High, But Remains Interesting
data.description:
November was good to Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF), as investor enthusiasm over programs like Gazyva in lupus, fenebrutinib in multiple sclerosis, and giredestrant in breast cancer fueled a 19% rally that certainly helped lessen the sting of a disappointing third quarter financial report that saw several key growth drivers slow noticeably (even if likely temporarily).

Roche shares have done well since my last update, with the ADRs up more than 35% against a roughly 22% positive move for the broader pharmaceutical space.

At this point, I’m a little more cautious on the shares. I do like management’s new approach to managing R&D and clinical development, and I believe it will increase productivity and returns over time. I also love the sheer scope and scale that the company offers. While internal R&D is usually the most cost-effective approach, Roche has the resources to buy its way into any therapeutic class it feels it needs.

The “but” is expectations and near-term drivers. A lot is riding on the upcoming persevERA study read-out for giredestrant, and disappointment here will likely be punished. Moreover, 2026 is otherwise looking thin for clinical catalysts, increasing the risk that the shares could lose institutional interest, particularly if/when rivals report strong clinical data for competing drugs. While still a good core long-term holding for patient investors, the risk/reward today may not be ideal for the next six months.

The Upcoming persevERA Read-Out Is A Big One

Giredestrant, Roche’s oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (or SERD), is one of the most significant compounds in the company’s late-stage pipeline. If clinical trials go well, this drug could generate over $7 billion in peak revenue, and possibly well over $10 billion if everything goes right, as it has the potential of becoming an “all comers” type of drug for the most common form of breast cancer.

Roche’s interim analysis of the Phase III lidERA study certainly helped stoke more enthusiasm. This study was designed to explore the use of the drug in a neoadjuvant setting for people with early-stage ER+ HER2- breast cancer, and the drug showed both a statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in disease-free survival versus endocrine therapy, as well as a clear positive trend in survival.

That’s certainly positive, but there are a few “buts” that need to be remembered. First, the control arm really doesn’t reflect the current standard of care (or at least not completely), as CDK4/6 drugs are now also in use. Thus, while the trial design ostensibly covers about 50% of the potential patient population, in “real-world use,” it may be more on the order of 20%.

With that in mind, there could be some risk when Roche reports the actual numbers (I believe they’ll be presenting at the SABCS conference that runs from Dec. 9 to Dec. 12). If giredesrant shows less than a 25% benefit to disease-free survival, it may well not be enough to satisfy rising expectations.

While adjuvant use is not a trivial opportunity (it could well exceed $10 billion/year in the 2030s), first-line use in metastatic disease is likely to be at least as large, and that’s what the perservERA study will address. Based upon early clinical work, it’s possible that giredestrant may work more broadly than competing SERDs from rivals like AstraZeneca (AZN) or Lilly (LLY), but clinical confirmation is needed. Given how the share price has responded since the positive lidERA press release, expectations are higher now, and Roche really needs this drug to come through with strong, clean data to support the momentum.

As Is Often The Case, The Pipeline Has Had Its Ups And Downs

This year (2025) has been a mixed one for Roche in terms of pipeline development. Looking at management’s list of key readouts from the time of the Q4’24 earnings call, astegolimab failed in its Phase III ARNASA COPD study, high-dose Ocrevus failed to show meaningful benefit, and results for vamikibart were mixed in uveitic macular edema (or UME), with one trial (MEERKAT) succeeding and one (SANDCAT) coming up short.

Clinical results for fenebrutinib were likewise mixed. While FENhance 2 showed significant reductions in relapses relative to Sanofi’s (SNY) Aubagio, the FENtrepid study only showed non-inferiority to Ocrevus in PPMS (though there was improvement starting from week 24). At this point, then, this is looking more like a “Ocrevus-extender” than a major step forward; not a bad drug, and certainly not a failure, but not meeting the most bullish hopes.

It hasn’t been all bad news, though. Giredestrant is looking good so far, and the ALLEGORY study of Gazyva/Gazyvaro in lupus (SLE, specifically) was a success, as was the INShore study in kids with idiopathic nephrotic syndrome. The company is also moving zosurabalpin into Phase III development for MDR bacterial infections, where it could become the first new drug for gram-negative bacteria in 50 years.

Looking at earlier-stage assets, a Phase Ib/IIa study of trontinemab showed significant plaque clearance (with 91% of patients becoming amyloid PET-negative), and the company is moving quickly into late-stage development for early symptomatic and pre-clinical Alzheimer’s indications. The company is also moving quickly into Phase III with NXT007, a new hemophilia A compound that holds promise for achieving hemostatic normalization (no bleeds) in a larger percentage of patients than Roche’s own drug Hemlibra or Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) Mim8.

Risks And Opportunities With A New Approach

As I discussed in my last article on Roche, I like how management has restructured its approach to R&D – culling drugs earlier in the process and using more rigorous criteria for advancing drugs, but also not dawdling when a drug seems meaningfully effective. I believe this has already upgraded the company’s later-stage pipeline assets, and I expect further improvements in R&D yield over the next five-plus years.

Roche has also been changing its approach regarding what it considers its addressable markets. Roche has long been a leader in relatively specialized fields like oncology, hematology, and neurology, but the company is now actively broadening its scope to more “general practice” medicines, including compounds for hypertension (its partnership with Alnylam (ALNY) on zilebesiran) and obesity.

Given the millions of people with these conditions, the revenue opportunities are huge. But then, so too is the scale of competition, as numerous major pharmas have followed Novo and Lilly (LLY) into this space over the last two years. These are also opportunities that will require a different marketing approach – while specialist conditions are more concentrated (fewer points of call), selling mass-market drugs requires a different approach, and it’s one where issues like pricing and access tend to be more significant.

By no means am I saying that Roche can’t succeed here; I’m simply pointing out that this is a notable change in strategy, and it will present some execution risks in the coming years.

The Outlook

Third-quarter results were not especially strong; while 7% constant-currency growth in the Pharma business and 2% growth in the Diagnostics business aren’t awful, both lines came in about 2% below expectations, and it was a noticeable slowdown from the double-digit Pharma growth in Q2. Likewise, while there are quarter-specific reasons why drugs like Ocrevus (up 6%) and Hemlibra (up 4%) decelerated, institutional investors often don’t care about the details and will bail out on stocks if they fear the main growth drivers are stalling.

The next few years are looking pretty solid for Roche. Relative to many large pharma companies, Roche has relatively little generics/biosimilar risk. I do see some competitive risks, though, including Novo’s Mim8 in hemophilia A (which is not yet approved, but has shown better bleed protection) and AstraZeneca/Daiichi Sankyo’s (OTCPK:DSNKY) Enhertu, which I do see potentially taking share from Roche’s Kadcyla and Perjeta over time.

I expect next year to be relatively similar to 2025 in terms of revenue growth (around 5%), and I’m looking for around 4% to 5% growth over the next five years, slowing toward 4% over the longer term. While major potential contributors like astegolimab and vamikibart may not look as robust as before, there are multiple drugs still in the pipeline with over $2 billion/year in peak revenue potential, with particular upside from names like giredestrant, trontinemab, zilebesiran, and NXT007 if trials go well.

On the margin side, I don’t expect too much turbulence, and I think the company will continue to produce EBITDA margins in the high 30% range and free cash flow margins in the low-to-mid-20% range on a pretty consistent basis over time. With that, I expect FCF growth a bit above revenue growth over the long term, but still in the 4%-5% range.

Between discounted cash flow and a growth-driven P/E approach (there’s a good historical correlation between five-year EPS growth and forward P/Es for pharma companies), I believe Roche is more or less at fair value today and priced for a long-term total annualized return in the 7% to 8% range.

The Bottom Line

I generally like to buy stocks like Roche when the prospective long-term return is in the double digits, but those opportunities don’t come around on any predictable schedule. With that, I think today’s prospective return is fine for investors with patience and a long-term orientation. For investors more interested in short-term performance, this may not be an ideal setup, as expectations for giredestrant have risen noticeably, and there may not be enough sentiment-moving readouts in 2026 apart from earlier-stage obesity assets to really stoke near-term momentum.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
